rulings.Case Title: PHOENIX MONROE VS. MAPLEBEAR INC. D/B/A INSTACART
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition To Enforce Arbitration Default, Stay Arbitration, And For Statutory Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for May 5, 2026, line 1. PETITIONER PHOENIX MONROE'S Petition To Enforce Arbitration Default, Stay Arbitration, And For Statutory Sanctions. Petitioner Phoenix Monroe's motion to enforce arbitration default and stay arbitration and for statutory sanctions is denied. Respondent Maplebear Inc. d/b/a Instacart remitted arbitration fees untimely. Pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure, section 1281.98, petitioner seeks to remove her dispute from arbitration and litigate it in this court. Petitioner also seeks sanctions under section 1281.99 for respondent's purported material breach. In Hohenshelt v. Superior Court (2025) 18 Cal.5th 310, 344, the Supreme Court explains that, "[U]nder general contract law principles as under section 1281.98, a drafting party cannot avoid discharging the other party's contractual duty to proceed in arbitration if it willfully withholds fees necessary to move arbitration forward. Conversely, if the drafting party acted in good faith, it may seek relief under the above statutes, and its claim should be evaluated under the usual principles in law and equity governing relief from forfeiture or default, including whether the other party has been prejudiced." Here, the evidence demonstrates that the late payment of the arbitration fees was not willful, grossly negligent, or fraudulent. There was no material breach of the arbitration agreement. Rather, the evidence demonstrates that respondent timely paid the initial $2,000 filing fee but, due to an invoice link error and other administrative difficulties, including the arbitrator's misdirection of subsequent communications, respondent encountered problems making the subsequent payment. (Flores Decl., pars. 4-10; McNeely Decl., pars. 2-6.) Petitioner also fails to show salient prejudice due to the late payment. Based on these circumstances, the court denies petitioner's motion in its entirety. (See Wilson v. TAP Worldwide, LLC (2025) 114 Cal.App.5th 1077, 1090 [where "processing delay" caused late payment, default was not willful, grossly negligent, or fraudulent, and sections 1281.98 and 1281.99 remedies did not apply].) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Gayle M. Kono
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for May 5, 2026, line 5. DEFENDANT SOON YE LOUIE'S Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement. The unopposed motion for good faith settlement is granted for good cause shown in the moving papers. This determination "shall bar any other joint tortfeasor or co-obligor from any further claims against the settling tortfeasor or co-obligor for equitable comparative contribution, or partial or comparative indemnity, based on comparative negligence or comparative fault." rulings.Case Title: TRAVIS FIELDS VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO POLICE DEPARTMENT
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Notice Of Motion And Motion And Motion For Leave To File Fourth Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for May 5, 2026, line 7. PLAINTIFF TRAVIS FIELDS' Motion And Motion For Leave To File Fourth Amended Complaint. Plaintiff Travis Fields's unopposed motion for leave to file a fourth amended complaint is denied without prejudice. Fields did not provide a copy of the proposed amendment and a statement of which allegations are to be added or deleted by page, paragraph, and line. (See Rule of Court 3.1324, subd. (a).) rulings.Rulings: On the Law and Motion / Discovery calendar for May 5, 2026, line 11. PLAINTIFF VINAY AWASTHI'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Discovery. Plaintiff Vinay K. Awasthi's motion to compel further responses to discovery is denied without prejudice. The court has sustained defendant's demurrer to Awasthi's first amended complaint without leave to amend. Awasthi has filed an appeal of this order. There are no pending claims against defendant in this court, and the entirety of the case is stayed by Awasthi's appeal.
